1:
The chapter opens with a name that will dominate the rest of the book, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry pauses over it. Here is the first mention of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, that head of gold, a potent prince and the terror of the mighty in the land of the living; yet Henry observes that his name would never have entered the sacred writ at all had he not been employed in the destruction of Jerusalem and the captivity of the Jews. He made Jehoiakim his tributary and held him in subjection three years — Nebuchadnezzar beginning his reign, Henry reckons, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim, and in his eighth year taking him prisoner and then restoring him upon a promise of faithfulness, a promise kept some three years before the king rebelled, probably in hope of aid from Egypt. Henry draws the moral that governs the whole reign: “If Jehoiakim had served his God as he should have done, he would not have been servant to the king of Babylon”; God would thus make him know the difference between his service and the service of the kings of the countries, as 2 Chron. 12:8 had warned.

The reformed John Gill sets the first coming early, in the latter end of the third or the beginning of the fourth year of Jehoiakim and the first of Nebuchadnezzar, when Jehoiakim was taken but restored on his promise of subjection, hostages being given — the occasion, he notes, on which Daniel and his companions were carried captive along with some of the vessels of the temple. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in the conqueror’s rise: Nebuchadnezzar, son of Nabopolassar, was sent by his father against the revolted provinces and took Carchemish and all that had belonged to the Egyptians from the Euphrates to the Nile; Jehoiakim, till then tributary to Necho king of Egypt, he attacked, reduced, and obliged to become tributary to Babylon, until at the end of three years he revolted.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dates the subjugation later, to Jehoiakim’s fifth or sixth year. In the fourth year Nebuchadnezzar had defeated Necho at Carchemish (Jer. 46:2) and was suddenly called home by news of his father’s death, succeeding him at Babylon in the same year (Jer. 25:1); and from Jer. 36:9 he gathers that towards the end of Jehoiakim’s fifth year the Babylonian invasion was expected. Then Nebuchadnezzar humbled Jehoiakim, who had probably submitted, putting him in chains and carrying off some of the temple treasures (2 Chron. 36:6-7), and left him on the throne as a vassal who paid tribute three years and then tried to throw off the yoke. So the witnesses converge on the fact of vassalage and revolt while dividing over its exact year, some placing the first blow at the third or fourth year with the deportation of Daniel, Ellicott a year or two later.
2:
When Jehoiakim broke his allegiance the raiders came — “the LORD sent against him bands of the Chaldees, and bands of the Syrians, and bands of the Moabites, and bands of the children of Ammon” — and the witnesses fasten first upon the striking subject of the sentence. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the Lord sent against Jehoiakim by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar, against whom he had rebelled. But the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the point that no mention is made here of any commission from the king of Babylon, only of the commission from the King of kings: the LORD sent against him all these bands. Many, Henry reflects, are serving God’s purposes who are not aware of it; the Chaldeans, Syrians, Moabites, and Ammonites were now in Babylon’s pay and yet were gratifying their own ancient enmity to the Israel of God, and above both stood the hand of God directing all.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the political scene: Jehoiakim’s revolt was doubtless instigated by Egypt, and while Nebuchadnezzar himself was engaged elsewhere in his empire, predatory bands of Chaldeans and of the neighbouring peoples — the hereditary enemies of Judah, who had submitted to Babylon and were nothing loth to make reprisals for the power Josiah had once exercised over them — ravaged the Judæan territory, the very “evil neighbours” of Jer. 12:8-17. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that these bands were of several nations, willing to fight under the banner of so puissant and victorious an emperor.

On the clause “according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servants the prophets,” the witnesses gather the whole prophetic testimony behind the invasion. Clarke points back to what Huldah had predicted (2 Kings 22:16) and to the oracles of Jeremiah 14–16. Ellicott lengthens the roll: Isaiah, Micah, Urijah (Jer. 26:20), Huldah, Jeremiah, Habakkuk, and doubtless others whose names have not come down, had all foretold the fate now closing in upon Judah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add the chronological frame, taking the “third year of Jehoiakim” of Daniel 1:1 as the point from which Nebuchadnezzar’s advance is reckoned: he smote Necho at Carchemish at the beginning of the fourth year (Jer. 46:2), took Jerusalem in the same year, made Jehoiakim tributary, and carried off both temple vessels and young men of royal blood as hostages, Daniel among them (2 Chron. 36:7; Dan. 1:2). The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the marauding bands, whatever their own motives and their earthly paymaster, were the instrument of a sentence long ago pronounced from heaven.
3:
“Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh” — and here the witnesses probe both the certainty of the decree and the question of whose guilt it answered. The reformed John Gill reads the verse as the sure and certain decree of God that his people should go into captivity, on account of which he stirred up the spirit of Nebuchadnezzar and gave him his orders. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the Hebrew more exactly — “only upon the mouth,” that is, at the command, of Jehovah did it happen in Judah — and notes that the Septuagint and Syriac read “wrath” instead of “mouth,” a reading Ewald preferred and which recurs at v.20. “Out of his sight,” he adds, means from before God’s face, or, as the Targum explains, from the land where he was present in his temple.

Keil and Delitzsch drive home the severity: no other reason is given than that the Lord had determined to put Judah away because of Manasseh’s sins (2 Kings 21:12-16; 2 Kings 23:27), and “Jehovah would not forgive” — even had the greatest intercessors, Moses and Samuel, stood before him, as Jer. 15:1 declares, because the measure of the sins was full and his holy righteousness had to punish.

Here a real difference opens. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley will not let “the sins of Manasseh” be read as though a later generation were punished for a dead king’s crimes: the people were judged, he insists, “properly and directly for their own sins, and occasionally for the sins of Manasseh, which had never been charged upon them if they had not made them their own by their repetition of them.” Against this, the grammatical-historical witnesses take the sins of Manasseh as the climax of Judah’s long provocation, the filled cup that at last drew down the sentence — so that Manasseh’s guilt, and not merely the present generation’s, is named as the ground. The debate is a genuine one over how the covenant justice works: whether the fathers’ sin is visited on the children who repeat it, the children bearing chiefly their own guilt, or whether the accumulated guilt of Manasseh’s reign is itself the reason the kingdom now falls.

Yet none of the witnesses makes the decree arbitrary. Gill grounds it in God’s settled purpose, Ellicott in the defilement of the land, Keil in the unforgiven fulness of the sin; and all agree that the coming of the bands was no accident of politics but the execution of a word the LORD had bound himself to keep. The removal of Judah “out of his sight” is the withdrawal of the sheltering presence that had made the temple and the land a refuge, now surrendered to the sentence of a righteous God.
4:
The ground of the sentence is pressed further — “and also for the innocent blood that he shed, for he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood, which the LORD would not pardon.” The reformed John Gill sees in it the long, cruel usage of the prophets and servants of the Lord, a shedding of righteous blood still crying out for reckoning. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase “blood of the innocent” and reports the guess of Thenius, that the murder of some prominent person such as Isaiah may be intended, and his wish to distinguish the two clauses of the verse — a distinction Ellicott rejects, since the closely parallel language of 2 Kings 21:16 does not favour it.

The weight of Ellicott’s comment falls on the theology of the words “which the LORD would not pardon,” literally “and Jehovah willed not to pardon.” He refuses to soften the statement of vv.3-4, as Bähr had done by claiming that the nation was punished not for Manasseh’s sins but only for its persistence in the same kind of sins. No, says Ellicott: the sins of Manasseh are regarded as the climax in Judah’s long course of provocation; the cup was full, and judgment ready to fall. It had only been suspended, not revoked, in the reign of the good king Josiah. The innocent blood shed by Manasseh cried to heaven for vengeance, and the ruin of the kingdom was the answer of the All-righteous Judge.

Ellicott meets head-on the objection that in this case children suffered for their fathers’ misdeeds. That, he answers, was precisely the Old Testament doctrine, until Ezekiel proclaimed another in Ezek. 18:19 — the principle already stated in Exod. 20:5 and Deut. 5:9 that God visits the fathers’ iniquity upon the children. Looking at the catastrophe from another standpoint, he adds, national iniquities must be chastised in the present life if at all, and the sufferings of the exile were necessary for the purification of Israel from its inveterate tendency to apostatize from Jehovah.

So the witnesses stand together on the fact — that unpardoned blood-guilt sealed Jerusalem’s doom — while Ellicott presses the harder question the verse raises, whether it is just that a later generation bear the reckoning of Manasseh’s murders. His answer is that the guilt was corporate and cumulative, the land itself defiled by blood, and that the fall of the kingdom, terrible as it was, served the LORD’s deeper purpose of cleansing a people he would not utterly abandon. The refusal to pardon is not caprice but the fixed response of holiness to blood that has never been atoned.
5:
The formal closing-notice of Jehoiakim’s reign — “now the rest of the acts of Jehoiakim, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah?” — draws only two witnesses, but they draw out both what the verse hides and what the parallel records supply. The reformed John Gill turns to the fuller notice in the Chronicles, where it is added that his “abominations, and that which was found in him” were also recorded; and beyond his rebellion against the king of Babylon and his shedding of innocent blood, Gill reports the interpretation (he cites Lyra) that this phrase points to idolatrous marks made upon his body for superstitious purposes.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers what may be learned of the king’s remaining deeds from the prophets. Assuming, with Hitzig, that Habakkuk 2:9-14 refers to Jehoiakim, he concludes that the king severely oppressed his people by exacting forced labour upon the defences of Jerusalem; and from Thenius he reports the further inference, drawn from the words “that he may set his nest on high,” that Jehoiakim strengthened and enlarged the fortress on Ophel which Manasseh had first erected — an oppression already indicted by the prophet in Jer. 22:13-17, where the building of a great house by unrighteousness is denounced.

Ellicott marks a quiet significance in the formula itself. This, he observes, is the last reference in the books of Kings to “the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah,” and from that fact Bähr concludes that the source-work did not extend beyond the reign of Jehoiakim — the annals of Judah’s kings breaking off here, as the kingdom itself hastens to its end. The witnesses do not contend; together they let the dry citation carry its own sombre weight. A king remembered chiefly for oppression, bloodshed, and idolatrous self-indulgence is dismissed to the common register of the kings, and even that register is now closing. The historian’s terse cross-reference, pointing the reader elsewhere for deeds he will not himself relate, becomes in Ellicott’s hands a small marker of the twilight of the monarchy: the last of the annals, for a throne that has nearly run its course.
6:
“So Jehoiakim slept with his fathers; and Jehoiachin his son reigned in his stead.” The bland formula sits strangely over so ruined a reign, and the witnesses converge on the ominous silence within it — the omitted notice of burial — and divide over how the king in fact died. The reformed John Gill states the paradox plainly: Jehoiakim died as his fathers died, but was not buried with them, and indeed had no burial at all, according to the prophecy of Jeremiah; falling into the hands of the king of Babylon, he was bound in chains to be carried to Babylon, but died as he came out of Jerusalem, was cast out at the gates, and lay unburied. Gill adds that at this time some of the temple vessels were carried away and set in the idol’s house at Babylon.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch defend the harmony of the verse with the prophet. That Jehoiakim “fell asleep with his fathers” does not contradict Jer. 22:19, “he shall be buried with the burial of an ass, drawn and cast forth beyond the gates of Jerusalem,” nor Jer. 36:30, that his dead body should be exposed to the heat by day and the frost by night; the formula speaks only of his death, not of any honour in it. Charles Ellicott presses the same point: the usual notice of the king’s burial is deliberately omitted, and the omission is significant read in the light of Jeremiah’s word. Jehoiakim, he judges, was slain in an encounter with the marauding bands of v.2, his body left to decay where it fell; and he records Ewald’s conjecture that the king was lured out of Jerusalem to a pretended conference with the Chaldeans, treacherously seized, and, proving a refractory prisoner, slain and denied all funeral honours, his family craving the restoration of the corpse in vain.

Against this reading of a violent, dishonoured end stands the caution of Thenius, which Ellicott reports: the words of the text, he alleged, imply merely death, without further qualification, and not necessarily a violent one. So the debate is drawn — whether “slept with his fathers” here conceals a bloody death and a body flung out unburied, as Gill, Ellicott, and Wesley suppose and as Keil’s defence of the prophecy allows, or whether, with Thenius, the phrase asserts no more than that the king died. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley sides firmly with the first: it is not said he was buried with his fathers, and no doubt Jeremiah’s prophecy was fulfilled that he should not be lamented but buried with the burial of an ass. Clarke turns rather to the successor, remarking that Jehoiachin, reigning but three months as a mere vassal, is scarcely to be reckoned a king at all — for Jeremiah had said Jehoiakim should have none to sit upon the throne of David (Jer. 36:30) — and noting that this Jehoiachin is the Jechonias of Matt. 1:11.
7:
The narrative lifts its eyes from Judah to the wider board of empire — “and the king of Egypt came not again any more out of his land: for the king of Babylon had taken, from the river of Egypt unto the river Euphrates, all that pertained to the king of Egypt.” The witnesses read the verse as the measure of Egypt’s collapse and of Judah’s consequent isolation. The reformed John Gill explains that Necho came no more out of his own land to gather the tribute he had laid on Judah or to help her kings, neither Jehoiakim nor Jehoiachin — not until the days of Zedekiah, and then he was forced to retire without giving the aid he came to bring.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the cause bluntly: Necho was so crushed by the Babylonians that he was obliged to confine himself within the limits of his own states and could no more attempt any conquest; the text itself tells how much he had lost. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as a deliberate notice of the political posture at the moment Jehoiachin succeeded his father. Necho, deprived by Nebuchadnezzar of all his conquests and so crippled that he durst not venture again beyond his own borders, could offer nothing; and thus Judah was left, denuded of all external help, to face the consequences of its revolt from Babylon, which speedily overtook it in the siege of v.10. “The river of Egypt,” he notes, is the torrent of the Wady-el-Arish; and though the details of the campaign are nowhere recorded, it is clear from the verse that before the battle of Carchemish Necho had made himself master of the whole of Syria and the country east of the Jordan.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the same reckoning forward, taking the verse as the seal upon Egypt’s withdrawal from the contest for Syria and the hinge that turns the story toward the young king now to be introduced. The witnesses do not divide. Together they draw the strategic lesson the historian intends: the one power to which Judah might have looked for rescue has been swept from the field, its dominion from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates stripped away, so that when Babylon’s army returns there will be no deliverer. Judah’s rebellion, begun in the hope of Egyptian arms, is left to reap the whirlwind alone; and the very completeness of Necho’s ruin is the dark guarantee that the sentence pronounced upon Jerusalem will now be carried through without hindrance from the south.
8:
The reign of Jehoiachin is introduced with the note of his age, and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catches at once its tragic brevity. This should have been the history of king Jehoiachin’s reign, he says, but alas it is only the history of his captivity; he came to the crown not to have the honour of wearing it but the shame of losing it — ideo tantum venerat, ut exiret, he came in only to go out. An unhappy young prince, thrust into a falling house and a sinking throne by an unnatural father who had left him nothing to inherit but his own miseries; yet Henry insists he reigned long enough to show that he justly smarted for his fathers’ sins, for he trod in their steps, doing evil in the sight of the LORD, and did nothing to cut off the entail of the curse.

The chief crux of the verse is the king’s age, and the witnesses divide over it. Our text makes Jehoiachin eighteen years old at his accession, but 2 Chron. 36:9 reads eight. The reformed John Gill offers a reconciliation by co-regency: Jehoiachin may have been made king by his father in the first year of that father’s reign, so that he was eight when he began to reign with him and eighteen when he began to reign alone. Gill records too the alternative of Dr. Lightfoot — that “the son of eight years” means Jehoiachin fell in the eighth year of the captivity of Judah — but he inclines, in the end, to think the number in Chronicles simply a copyist’s slip of eight for eighteen, the more so as the Arabic and Syriac there read eighteen. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley holds firmly to the co-regency: in his eighth year Jehoiachin began to reign with his father, who made him king with himself as divers kings of Israel and Judah had done in times of trouble, and in his eighteenth he reigned alone.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke will have none of the harmonizing and pronounces the lower number a plain error. Jehoiachin, he argues, cannot have been eight, for having reigned but three months he was carried to Babylon and there had wives, which a child of eight or nine could not; nor could it truly be said of so young a child that he did evil, as a king, in the sight of the LORD; the place in Chronicles, he concludes, must be corrupt. So the debate stands between reconciliation by a co-regency, which saves both figures, and frank emendation, which corrects the eight of Chronicles to the eighteen of Kings.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, leaving the arithmetic aside, dwells on the names: Jehoiachin means “Jah will confirm,” and the king is variously called Jeconiah and, in Jer. 22:24, the shortened and ominous Coniah. His mother Nehushta’s name he refers perhaps to her complexion, and her father Elnathan he identifies with one of Jehoiakim’s princes named in Jer. 26:22.
9:
“And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father had done” — the standing verdict falls on Jehoiachin as on his fathers, and the witnesses ask how a boy-king reigning but three months could have earned it. The reformed John Gill answers from the co-regency he has argued: being partner with his father upon the throne, Jehoiachin was partner in his sins and continued in them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply register the judgment and press on to the siege, marking that “at that time” — when Jehoiachin had come to the throne, or, as 2 Chron. 36:10 puts it, “at the turn of the year,” in the spring — the servants of Nebuchadnezzar came up against Jerusalem.

Charles Ellicott raises a question that has divided the commentators: whether the young lion of Ezek. 19:5-9, who learned to catch the prey and devoured men, is Jehoiachin. Keil and Ewald, he reports, refer the passage to him; but Thenius objects, asking how in his position, and in a reign of some ninety days, much of which must be allowed for the siege, Jehoiachin could possibly have done what Ezekiel there describes; and Hitzig, with Thenius, refers the oracle rather to Zedekiah. The debate turns on whether so brief and hemmed-in a reign can carry the weight of the prophet’s fierce image, or whether it belongs to the last king instead.

Against the verdict of Scripture Ellicott sets a dissenting ancient voice: Josephus calls Jehoiachin “naturally good and just,” a judgment Ellicott thinks rests on a misunderstanding of the tender words of Jer. 22:24, 28, where the LORD’s grief over Coniah has been mistaken for praise of his character. The witnesses themselves do not soften the evaluation; whether Jehoiachin’s guilt was gathered in a co-regency with his father, as Gill holds, or simply inherited and briefly displayed, the inspired formula binds him to the evil of the house from which he sprang. His three months on a doomed throne are long enough for Scripture to write over him the same sentence it wrote over Jehoiakim, and the historian moves at once from the verdict to its execution, as the armies of Babylon appear beneath the walls. The judgment is not lengthened by the reign; it is sealed by the lineage.
10:
“At that time the servants of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came up against Jerusalem, and the city was besieged.” The reformed John Gill fixes the moment tersely — “at that time,” when Jehoiachin reigned — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens it to the spring of the year, following 2 Chron. 36:10. From Jer. 13:19, that “the cities of the south are shut up,” Ellicott reports the inference of Thenius that Nebuchadnezzar drew a cordon across that part of the country to cut off any succour from Egypt — Judah, as the last verse showed, having no help to expect from that quarter in any case. The “servants,” he notes, are the generals of the king, as the word is used in 2 Kings 19:6; and the passive “was besieged” will recur in the fuller account of the final siege at 2 Kings 25:2.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the siege under the shadow of the covenant curse. Nebuchadnezzar, who had first only sent his forces to ravage the country in v.2, now comes in person and lays siege to the city; and in this Henry hears the fulfilment of the ancient threat of Deut. 28:49, that the LORD would bring against a disobedient people a nation from far, of fierce countenance, which should besiege them in all their gates. What Moses had warned a thousand years before is now at the walls of Jerusalem.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill marks the timing, Ellicott the strategy, Henry the theology, and together they render the moment when the harassment of raiding bands hardens into a formal siege. The generals of Babylon come up first, drawing their lines about the city and sealing the southern roads, and the young king within has no ally, no fortress deep enough, no help from Egypt to break the ring. What began as a punitive expedition against a rebellious vassal has become the encirclement foretold in the Law, the first of the two great sieges that will bring the city down; and the historian, having named the besiegers, will turn in the next verse to the arrival of Nebuchadnezzar himself and the surrender that follows. The gathering of the enemy about Jerusalem is at once a fact of war and, to the witnesses, a word of God kept to the letter.
11:
“And Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came against the city, and his servants did besiege it.” The verse marks the arrival of the great king in person, and the witnesses, few and brief, attend chiefly to the sequence of events it records. The reformed John Gill notes simply that Nebuchadnezzar came in person, following his army — the generals had opened the siege, and now the sovereign himself appears before the walls.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the order from the Hebrew. “Did besiege” should be rendered “were besieging”: the king arrived after the siege had already begun, his servants having drawn their lines about the city before he came. And “came against,” he adds, is more exactly “came unto” — Nebuchadnezzar coming up to a leaguer already in progress rather than opening it himself. Keil and Delitzsch draw out the reason for his coming: during the siege the king came in person to punish Jehoiakim’s revolt in the person of his successor. The rebellion had been Jehoiakim’s, but Jehoiakim was dead; and so the reckoning for the broken allegiance now falls upon the son who inherited the throne but not the guilt of the revolt — a vicarious stroke that the witnesses feel keenly, the son answering with his crown for the father’s perfidy.

The witnesses do not divide, and the very economy of their comment matches the verse. It is a moment of quiet menace: the encircling army is now crowned by the presence of its emperor, the man whom Henry had called the terror of the mighty, come up from Babylon to see the sentence executed with his own eyes. That he comes to punish a rebellion its author never lived to answer for gives the scene its grim justice — the house of Josiah paying, king after king, for a course of sin and folly none of them would break off. The stage is set for the surrender: the city ringed, the king of Babylon at its gates, and within, a boy of eighteen with no counsel left but to yield. The historian holds the moment only long enough to name the besieger before the gates of Jerusalem swing open in the verse that follows, and the young king goes out to meet his conqueror.
12:
“And Jehoiachin the king of Judah went out to the king of Babylon, he, and his mother, and his servants, and his princes, and his officers.” The surrender is the pivot of the chapter, and the witnesses weigh both its prudence and its shame. The reformed John Gill explains that the young king went out not to fight but to submit, to surrender the city and cast himself upon the conqueror’s mercy. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that Jehoiachin, seeing it useless to defend himself any longer, gave himself up in hope of obtaining better terms.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the calculation and its disappointment. Jehoiachin went out to yield himself because he saw the impossibility of holding the city, and probably hoped to secure Nebuchadnezzar’s favour and even to keep his throne as a vassal by a voluntary submission; but Nebuchadnezzar showed him no such favour as he had shown Jehoiakim at the first taking of the city, treating him instead as a rebel, making him prisoner, and leading him away to Babylon with his mother, his wives, his princes and his chamberlains, as Jeremiah had foretold in Jer. 22:24, in the eighth year of Nebuchadnezzar’s reign. Charles Ellicott adds that the queen-mother and all the grandees and courtiers accompanied the king, who threw himself upon the clemency of Nebuchadnezzar, and that the “eighth year” of the taking exactly tallies with the data of Jer. 25:1 and Jer. 46:2.

Where the grammatical-historical witnesses read policy, the puritan-evangelical and wesleyan-arminian voices read a failure of faith. Matthew Henry laments that Jehoiachin, hearing that the king of Babylon had come in person, beat a parley and went out and surrendered at discretion; had he made his peace with God and taken the course Hezekiah took in the like case, he need not have feared the king of Babylon, but might have held out with courage, honour, and success, since one should have chased a thousand — but, wanting the faith and piety of an Israelite, he had not the resolution of a man, a soldier, or a prince, and he and his royal house delivered themselves up as prisoners of war, the consequence of their being servants of sin. John Wesley strikes a nearly identical note, that had Jehoiachin possessed the faith of an Israelite he might have held out with courage and honour; yet Wesley grants, more mildly, that the surrender was made “by the counsel of Jeremiah, and to his own good,” and adds the chronological caution that the “eighth year” is Nebuchadnezzar’s, not Jehoiachin’s, since Jehoiachin reigned not half a year.

So the witnesses divide less over the fact than over its verdict: whether the surrender was the only wise course for a city that could not be held, as Gill, Clarke, and the grammatical-historical writers judge, or a cowardly forsaking of the God who could have defended it, as Henry and Wesley charge. Yet even Wesley concedes that yielding was, in the event, the counsel of the prophet and to the king’s own good — so that the very act which betrayed his want of faith was overruled to spare his life.
13:
“And he carried out thence all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king’s house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the LORD.” The witnesses follow the plunder into the sanctuary and reckon its place in a longer history of spoliation. The reformed John Gill pictures the gates thrown open and the conqueror entering to strip both temple and palace of their riches. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the act in sequence, counting three several spoilings of the temple by Nebuchadnezzar: the first under Jehoiakim, when he bore off the greater part of the treasures and placed the vessels in the house of his god (Dan. 1:2), the vessels afterward profaned by Belshazzar (Dan. 5:2) and at last restored to the returning exiles by Cyrus (Ezra 1:2); the second now, under Jehoiachin, when he cut the remaining vessels in pieces; and the third under Zedekiah, when he carried off the brass and the brazen pillars, as told in 2 Kings 25:13-17.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the verb: to “cut in pieces” is to break the gold off the vessels, as in 2 Kings 16:17 — Nebuchadnezzar stripping the plating from the temple furniture rather than shattering the solid pieces, the smaller vessels of solid gold having been taken at the first capture and delivered up again in the days of Cyrus (Ezra 1:7). Charles Ellicott presses a historical puzzle: it is surprising that anything was left in the treasury after so many spoliations, and this, he thinks, both indicates the existence of secret store-chambers and lends support to the Chronicler’s account of the great wealth once stored in the sanctuary. The gold-plating, he adds, was now stripped from such vessels as the altar of incense, the table of shewbread, and the ark; and all of it fell out “as the LORD had said” — the very word spoken to Hezekiah in 2 Kings 20:17, that all in his house should be carried to Babylon.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on that fulfilment: now the word of God by Isaiah was accomplished, that all should be carried to Babylon; the vessels Solomon had made and laid up in store were cut off from the temple, and Nebuchadnezzar, beginning to break them in pieces, reserved them at last for his own use, so that Belshazzar was found drinking his wine from them. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley marvels that these golden vessels had survived the rifling of the temple by earlier kings of Egypt and of Judah — preserved, he supposes, by the care of the priests who hid them, or the clemency of conquerors, or the special providence of God — only to be swept away at last. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the emptying of the treasuries was the visible seal of a long-spoken prophecy, and that the vessels of the LORD’s house passing into a pagan temple were both the shame of Judah and the vindication of God’s word.
14:
“And he carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths.” The first great deportation is before us, and the witnesses begin by qualifying that sweeping “all.” The reformed John Gill takes “all Jerusalem” to mean the chief of its inhabitants — the more honourable, the rich, the useful — not every individual, for the poorer sort were left behind. Keil and Delitzsch agree, and add a moral note from Jer. 24:1-10: those carried off were not only the most affluent but the best portion of the people, the good figs of the prophet’s vision, while the refuse was left in the land.

The chief question the verse raises is who these deportees were, and here the witnesses divide over “the mighty men of valour.” The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads them straightforwardly as fighting men, reckoning seven thousand men of might and a thousand craftsmen and smiths; John Wesley concurs, distributing the ten thousand of this verse into the seven thousand mighty men and one thousand smiths of v.16, with the remaining two thousand made up of the princes and officers. Against this, Charles Ellicott reports that Thenius and Bähr prefer to understand not warriors but men of property and artisans, as the same phrase is taken in 2 Kings 15:20. Ellicott himself entertains both senses, and the debate is a real one: whether Nebuchadnezzar deported Jerusalem’s soldiery or its men of substance and skill.

On the craftsmen and smiths the witnesses are more nearly agreed, and see in their removal a deliberate disarming of the city. Ellicott explains that the craftsmen were workers in wood, stone, and metal, and the “smiths” — literally “those who shut” — were makers of the bolts and bars of doors and gates; by carrying them off Nebuchadnezzar made further revolt impossible, as the Philistines had once disarmed Israel in 1 Samuel 13:19. He pauses to dismiss Kimchi’s curious notion that the “smiths” were learned men who shut other people’s mouths with riddles none could solve. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the whole deportation as policy and providence together: Nebuchadnezzar carried off the strength of the city to weaken it, to secure his dominion and prevent revolt, and to enrich himself with the service of the captives; among them went the military men who could no longer defend their country, and the craftsmen and smiths whose removal, in effect, disarmed it. Henry recalls that some had already been carried away eight years before, in the third of Jehoiakim, Daniel and his fellows among them (Dan. 1:1), and that in this later deportation went the prophet Ezekiel (Ezek. 1:1) and, later, Mordecai (Esth. 2:6). The poorest sort, Ellicott notes from Jer. 39:10, those with neither property nor craft, were all that remained; and the witnesses leave a stripped and defenceless city, its leaders, warriors, and artisans marched away to Babylon.
15:
“And he carried away Jehoiachin to Babylon, and the king’s mother, and the king’s wives, and his officers, and the mighty of the land, those carried he into captivity from Jerusalem to Babylon.” The verse names the royal captives, and the witnesses mark both the fulfilment of prophecy in it and the length of the exile it began. The reformed John Gill notes soberly that Jehoiachin was carried to Babylon, where he continued at least thirty-seven years — the whole remainder of his life a captivity, as the close of the next chapter will record.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the verb here differs from that of v.14, so that the verse may be rendered “yea, he carried away” — this verse and the next giving the particulars of what the previous verse had stated in sum. In the general “princes” of that verse the persons are now defined: the king himself, the queen-mother, the royal wives, the court officers, and the magnates of the land, all of whom, Ellicott adds, must have taken refuge within Jerusalem at the approach of the Chaldean army only to be swept up together in its fall. That the king and his mother were led away, he notes, is the exact fulfilment of the word spoken over Coniah in Jer. 22:24-27, that the LORD would cast him and the mother that bore him into a strange country to die.

Keil and Delitzsch carry the same list forward toward the appointment of a new king in the verses to follow, treating this deportation of the royal house as the clearing of the throne that Nebuchadnezzar will presently fill with a puppet of his own. The witnesses do not divide. Together they register the removal of the whole reigning family — king, mother, wives, and officers — from Jerusalem to Babylon, the visible undoing of the house of David’s independent rule and the exact accomplishment of what Jeremiah had threatened. The dynasty is not destroyed, for Jehoiachin will live long in Babylon and be lifted from prison at the last; but its glory is stripped away, its members led captive, and its city left to a vassal-king. The verse is the formal record of a monarchy carried into exile, the shepherd taken with the flock, and the throne of David emptied to be filled at a conqueror’s pleasure.
16:
“And all the men of might, even seven thousand, and craftsmen and smiths a thousand, all that were strong and apt for war, even them the king of Babylon brought captive to Babylon.” The verse itemizes the ten thousand, and the two witnesses at hand work out its arithmetic and wrestle with a discrepancy of numbers. The reformed John Gill takes the figures as the particulars of the ten thousand already named, of whom seven thousand were the principal men of the land.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the fuller reckoning. The “men of might” are the mighty men of valour of v.14; and since these were seven thousand, and the craftsmen and smiths a thousand, and the whole number of exiles ten thousand, there remain two thousand belonging to the aristocratic classes — the princes and officers. But Jer. 52:28 gives a total of only three thousand and twenty-three, and Ellicott reports the explanation of Thenius, that the discrepancy arose from a transcriber’s confusion of a large yod, standing for ten, with a gimel, standing for three; while Josephus reached a total of ten thousand eight hundred and thirty-two by adding the eight hundred and thirty-two of the second deportation in Jer. 52:29 to the ten thousand here.

Ellicott lingers, too, over the phrase “all that were strong and apt for war,” literally “the whole, warriors and doers of battle.” Thenius takes it to mean that the eight thousand were all men in their prime and trained to arms; but Ellicott offers another sense, that “strong,” or “heroes,” may denote the seven thousand as actual fighting men, while “makers of war” describes the craftsmen as those employed in forging weapons and building defences — the Syriac, he notes, reading “all the men that made war.” The witnesses do not divide sharply, and the point on which they meet is the completeness of the stroke. Whether the numbers are reconciled by a scribal slip or by combining the deportations, and whether the last clause describes soldiers or armourers, the sense is the same: Nebuchadnezzar carried off precisely those in whom the city’s power of resistance lay, the strong and the skilled together, and left Jerusalem incapable of war. The careful counting of the captives is itself a kind of eloquence, numbering out the manhood and craft of a nation as it is led away, so that what remains behind can neither fight nor forge, and the throne can be handed to whatever vassal the conqueror pleases to set up.
17:
“And the king of Babylon made Mattaniah his father’s brother king in his stead, and changed his name to Zedekiah.” The conqueror sets a vassal on the throne, and the witnesses attend to the man, his kinship, and the ominous new name. The reformed John Gill identifies Mattaniah as the third son of Josiah, and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke as the son of Josiah and brother to Jehoiakim, whose name Nebuchadnezzar changed to Zedekiah. Charles Ellicott fills out the pedigree: Mattaniah was Josiah’s third son and full brother of Jehoahaz-Shallum (2 Kings 23:31); and since Jehoiachin was childless at the time (Jer. 22:30), though he had offspring later in the exile, his uncle had the first claim to the throne.

Keil and Delitzsch tell the same, marking that over the lower classes of the people left behind Nebuchadnezzar set the captive king’s paternal uncle, only ten years old when Josiah died and twenty-one at his accession, who as uncle of the eighteen-year-old Jehoiachin stood nearest the throne. But the witnesses find the heart of the verse in the changing of the name. Ellicott explains that Mattaniah, “gift of Jah,” was renamed Zedekiah, “Jah is righteousness” or “my righteousness”; and he hears behind the change the prophecy of Jer. 23:1-9, which denounces the shepherds that destroy and scatter the flock and promises a king whose name shall be “Jehovah is our righteousness” — the delusive hopes fastened on Zedekiah’s elevation answered, at last, only in the true Branch. Nebuchadnezzar’s clemency in setting a native prince on the throne, Ellicott suggests, may have fulfilled a promise made at the city’s surrender.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the new name as a warning aimed at its bearer: it was given to admonish Zedekiah of the justice of God, which had so severely punished Jehoiakim for his rebellion and would no less certainly overtake him should he prove guilty of the same perfidiousness. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the change into a lesson on the humiliation of the kingdom. To remind Zedekiah, and to let all the world know, that he was his creature, the king of Babylon changed his name; God had once charged upon his people, “they have set up kings, but not by me” (Hos. 8:4), and now, to punish them for it, the king of Babylon shall have the setting up of their kings. The very name Zedekiah, signifying the justice of the LORD, Henry takes as a presage of the glorifying of God’s justice in his ruin. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the puppet-king’s borrowed name was both a token of Judah’s servitude and a silent prophecy of the righteous judgment that his own perfidy would bring to pass.
18:
“Zedekiah was twenty and one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. And his mother’s name was Hamutal, the daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah.” With the last king of Judah introduced, the two witnesses at hand set his age and lineage in order and note the parallel record that runs beside this closing chapter. The reformed John Gill reckons from the figure that Zedekiah was but between nine and ten years old when his father Josiah died — for Jehoahaz had reigned three months, Jehoiakim eleven years, and Jehoiachin three months and ten days — so that the whole troubled sequence of Josiah’s sons and grandson is compressed into little more than a decade before this youngest brother comes to the throne.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the fuller name and add a careful note on the sources. Hamutal, Zedekiah’s mother, was the daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah — not the prophet, but another of the name. And they observe that the historical appendix to the book of Jeremiah, Jeremiah 52, furnishes a close parallel to this section, agreeing with it for the most part word for word, save that it omits the murder of Gedaliah and the flight of the people to Egypt told in 2 Kings 25:22-26, and adds instead a reckoning of the numbers led away to Babylon in Jer. 52:28-30. From the further notices in that chapter concerning the temple vessels and the ornaments of the brazen pillars, which appear nowhere in the books of Kings, they conclude that neither text was borrowed from the other; both drew on a common source.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they frame the opening of Zedekiah’s eleven years with a genealogist’s exactness and a critic’s care, fixing the boy who had watched three kinsmen fall in a decade now raised, at twenty-one, to a throne that Babylon had emptied and would soon overthrow. The naming of his mother and her town, the counting of his years, the quiet cross-reference to the parallel in Jeremiah — all of it has the sound of a record being carefully closed, the last regnal formula of the house of David in Jerusalem set down with the same measured hand that had opened the reigns of happier kings. The stage is set, in the barest of terms, for the verdict and the rebellion that the final verses will bring.
19:
“And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim had done.” The familiar sentence falls on the last king as on the first of these brothers, and the witnesses mark both the obstinacy it reveals and the contemporary testimony that confirms it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke cries out at the wonder of it: how astonishing that not one of these kings takes warning by the judgments of God which had fallen on their sinful predecessors — the ruin of one brother teaching nothing to the next.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns to the prophets for the character of the reign. The evidence of Jeremiah, he says, should be set beside this verdict — the bad figs of Jer. 24:8, the king who would not hearken in Jer. 37:1-2, the weak prince overborne by his princes in Jer. 38:5 — and the contemporary state of religion is vividly reflected in the pages of Ezekiel, who moreover denounces Zedekiah’s breach of faith with the king of Babylon in Ezek. 17:11-21. That the verdict compares him with Jehoiakim, and not with Jehoiachin, Ellicott notes, is because Jehoiachin had reigned but three months, too briefly to serve as the measure of a reign.

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the theological knot in the verse that follows and read it back into this one. It was not, they insist with the older expositors, that God made Zedekiah wicked; rather that such a God-resisting man — simple, dependent upon his counsellors, yet despising the word of God and impenitent, as 2 Chron. 36:12-13 describes him — became king, and so became the cause of Jerusalem’s destruction. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Zedekiah’s evil was his own, freely chosen against the plainest warnings and the living voice of Jeremiah and Ezekiel at his side, and that the tragedy of the house of David is precisely this incorrigibility: king after king repeating the sins that had destroyed the last, until the throne itself was swept away. The verdict is no mere formula but the settled judgment of heaven upon a man who had every reason to fear and would not, and whose obstinacy, the next verse will show, hurried his kingdom to its end.
20:
“For through the anger of the LORD it came to pass in Jerusalem and Judah, until he had cast them out from his presence, that Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.” The chapter ends on the sentence that seals the city’s doom, and the witnesses read in it both the sin of the king and the wrath of God working through it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the rebellion in the eighth year of Zedekiah’s reign and reproves him sharply for it: he had violated the oath he swore to the king of Babylon, as 2 Chron. 36:13 charges, and this perjured revolt was the filling up of the measure of iniquity, upon which the wrath of God descends to the uttermost — the catastrophe itself reserved for the next chapter.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the difficult syntax. The verse means, literally, that “upon the anger of Jehovah it befell Jerusalem” — that which fell upon the city and Judah like a ruinous disaster was the evil-doing of Zedekiah named in v.19; and that such a prince should have been raised to the throne at all was itself a token of divine displeasure, his character being such as to hasten the final ruin. Ellicott would put a full stop after “presence,” reading the last clause as a fresh statement: “And Zedekiah rebelled.” He fills in the politics of that revolt from the prophets — Zedekiah looked for help to Pharaoh Hophra of Egypt (Ezek. 17:15; Jer. 37:5), and the neighbouring peoples of Edom, Ammon, Moab, Tyre, and Zidon, eager to throw off the Babylonian yoke, had proposed a general rising in Jer. 27:3. Jeremiah, he notes, opposed the whole project to the utmost of his power, and the event proved him right.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the mystery of the divine anger working through human sin: the people’s own sins, he says, were the true cause why God gave them wicked kings, whom he suffered to do wickedly, that they might bring the long-deserved and threatened punishments upon themselves and their people. The witnesses do not finally divide. Clarke fastens on the broken oath, Ellicott on the doomed folly of the revolt, Wesley on the sins of the people that lay beneath it all; but together they close the chapter where it began, with the anger of the LORD directing the affairs of kings and empires to the appointed end. Zedekiah’s rebellion is at once his own perjured act and the instrument by which God casts a guilty people out from his presence — the last free choice of the last king of Judah, and the last turn of the sentence long ago pronounced.
